results.title: $70 Million AI.com Sale: The Largest Domain Transaction in History
results.text:
$70 Million AI.com Sale: The Largest Domain Transaction in History

Agree & Join LinkedIn

By clicking Continue to join or sign in, you agree to LinkedIn’s User Agreement, Privacy Policy, and Cookie Policy.

The technology world has been left stunned by a record-breaking digital asset deal that has redefined the value of the internet. As reported by Finance Magnates, the domain name AI.com has been sold for an incredible $70 million. This monumental transaction is now considered the biggest domain sale in the history of the world, marking a massive shift in how investors perceive the value of artificial intelligence. The buyer, Kris Marszalek, who is the CEO of Crypto.com, has made a decisive move to secure his place at the forefront of the AI revolution. This acquisition signals a major pivot for blockchain leaders who are now pouring capital into the AI ecosystem to capture global attention.

Premium domain names have always been high-value assets, but a $70 million price tag is a historic milestone. It easily surpasses the previous $30 million record set by Voice.com. For a business leader like Marszalek, owning AI.com is not just about a website; it is about owning the most valuable category identifier on the planet. As every major corporation looks for ways to integrate machine learning, the two letters "AI" have become the most expensive digital real estate in the history of the world. By acquiring this domain, Marszalek is positioning his brand to dominate the consumer AI space, moving far beyond the specific niche of cryptocurrency.

### Evaluating the $70M Investment Against Global Records

To understand the gravity of this $70 million deal, we must compare it with past sales recorded in the history of the world. While some business buyouts like Cars.com are valued higher, those included thousands of employees and massive physical assets. In terms of a "pure domain name" transaction, AI.com now holds the undisputed crown. Data from authoritative sources like DNJournal confirms that no other single URL has commanded such a high price in a direct cash or crypto trade. This is a clear indicator that digital assets are now seen as stable and highly appreciating stores of value.

The financial journey of this specific domain is equally fascinating. Only a few years ago, AI.com was rumored to be worth around $11 million when OpenAI was the primary user. The jump to $70 million in such a short period is one of the most profitable appreciations ever seen in the history of the world. This massive increase in value reflects a global gold rush toward AI-focused branding. For Marszalek, this is a long-term strategic play that secures immediate trust and authority in an increasingly competitive market.

### Kris Marszalek and the Global Vision for Consumer AI

Kris Marszalek has a reputation for high-impact marketing and a far-reaching vision of world technological trends. He successfully built

results.title: AI.com sold for $70 million in one of the biggest domain deals ever
results.text:
AI.com sold for $70 million in one of the biggest domain deals ever - Storyboard18

- DNPA
- Global Pioneers Summit
- Storyboard18 Awards For Creativity
- The Visionaries
- Power of Purpose
- Share the Spotlight
- Digital Entertainment Summit
- DNPA

# AI.com sold for $70 million in one of the biggest domain deals ever

## AI.com has been sold for $70 million to Crypto.com CEO Kris Marszalek in one of the largest publicly disclosed domain deals.

By Storyboard18| February 13, 2026, 17:19:29 IST

Follow us

Two letters. $70 million. The AI.com deal explained

What began as a quiet domain purchase into one of the most valuable deals in internet history.

Malaysian entrepreneur Arsyan Ismail bought AI.com years ago simply because the letters matched his initials.

The global boom in generative AI, machine learning tools and consumer-facing artificial intelligence platforms dramatically reshaped the domain’s worth.

As startups and established companies rushed to position themselves in the AI race, the term “AI” became one of the most searched and commercially powerful technology keywords.

Industry observers say this shift transformed AI.com from an overlooked purchase into a strategic digital asset, offering instant brand recall and credibility in a crowded market. Two-letter domain names are some of the scarcest assets online. With just 676 possible combinations in total, the majority were secured early in the internet era and seldom come up for sale.

Also Read: Elon Musk criticises Anthropic after $30 billion funding round lifts valuation

In April 2025, Ismail sold AI.com for $70 million, around Rs 634 crore, making it the highest publicly disclosed domain sale to date.

The buyer was Kris Marszalek, Chief Executive Officer of Crypto.com. Reports said the deal was completed entirely in cryptocurrency.

The previous publicly known record for a domain sale was set in 2010, when CarInsurance.com was sold for $49.7 million.

AI.com returned to the spotlight during Super Bowl LX, when Crypto.com unveiled a new “agentic AI” platform under the AI.com banner. The platform was showcased as offering personal AI agents capable of sending messages, trading stocks and handling tasks across applications.

Marszalek has said that securing AI.com was critical to ensuring his company would not be “commoditised” amid intensifying competition in the AI sector.

Reports suggest Ismail had earlier paid around $10 million to acquire the domain from a domain portfolio firm. Selling it for $70 million implies a profit of roughly $60 million. Since the payment was reportedly made in cryptocurrency, the final realised value may vary depending on market conditions.

First Published on February 13, 2026, 16:29:33 IST

## More from Storyboard18

### Digital

### What is ‘vibe coding’ and why Sundar Pichai, Sridhar Vembu are debating it

### Brand Marketing

results.title: Ai.com purchase price was $70M - TLD Investors
results.author: Raymond Hackney
results.text:
Ai.com purchase price was $70M - TLD Investors 

Larry Fischer of GetYourDomain.com announced on LinkedIn that the purchase price for Ai.com was $70M. The story got picked up by The Financial Times. Buyer is the owner of Crypto.com.

Kris Marszalek Co-Founder of Crypto.com posted on X that he acquired the name in April of last year.

I purchased https://t.co/ac2AqjBNxj in April. Since that time, we created a team that has been steadily building. There are always twists and turns, but I’m excited with our first launch this Sunday during the Super Bowl. pic.twitter.com/BbqVo1bQLZ

> — Kris (@kris) February 6, 2026

The founder of cryptocurrency trading site Crypto.com has bought AI.com for $70mn, the highest price ever disclosed for a domain name sale, to launch a new entrant to the artificial intelligence race. Kris Marszalek, co-founder and chief executive of Crypto.com, plans to debut ai.com in a Super Bowl ad this weekend. The site will offer a personal “AI agent” that consumers can use to send messages, use apps and trade stocks.

results.title: AI.com's $70M Sale Shatters Records, Redefines Digital Real Estate
results.author:
GF
         
         
           George Flores 
             
               George Flores
results.text:
AI.com's $70M Sale Shatters Records, Redefines Digital Real Estate - BriefGlance.com 

 

 

 

 

# AI.com Sells for Record $70 Million, Reshaping Digital Asset Valuations

NEW YORK, NY – February 09, 2026 – In a transaction that redefines the value of digital real estate, the domain name AI.com has been acquired for a record-shattering $70 million. The sale, exclusively brokered by advisory firm GetYourDomain.com, more than doubles the previous industry benchmark and firmly establishes two-letter .com domains tied to transformative technologies as a new class of ultra-premium assets.

The buyer is Kris Marszalek, the founder and CEO of the prominent cryptocurrency platform Crypto.com. The seller, Arsyan Ismail, had held the domain since 1993, having originally acquired it because the initials matched his own. The deal represents an astronomical return on investment and a pivotal moment in the history of internet commerce, underscoring the immense strategic value placed on category-defining digital identities.

## A New Benchmark for Digital Real Estate

The $70 million price tag for AI.com obliterates the previous public record for a domain-only sale, the $30 million paid for Voice.com in 2019. It also surpasses other landmark deals like the $49.7 million sale of CarInsurance.com in 2010. This transaction dramatically resets valuation expectations in the domain market, particularly for assets considered irreplaceable.

"This sale marks a defining moment for digital real estate," said Larry Fischer, Founder of GetYourDomain.com, who brokered the deal. "AI.com is not just a domain name — it represents the front door to one of the most transformative technologies of our generation. The buyer recognized that owning AI.com means owning the category itself."

The sale punctuates a clear trend of escalating values for premium domains, especially those that are short, memorable, and aligned with booming industries. While the domain market is notoriously opaque, with some estimates suggesting over 90% of sales go unreported, this public, record-setting transaction provides a rare and powerful benchmark. It signals a strategic shift among tech leaders and global brands, who now view the ownership of such singular digital assets not as a marketing expense, but as a permanent competitive advantage and a core pillar of their long-term strategy. The payment for the domain, made entirely in cryptocurrency, also bridges the worlds of digital currency and digital real estate, highlighting the evolving nature of high-value asset transactions.

## The Strategic Play for Category Dominance

For buyer Kris Marszalek, the acquisition of AI.com is a move straight from a well-established playbook. As the architect behind Crypto.com, he demonstrated a keen understanding of the power of a category-defining brand. His company, originally launched as "Monaco," made a strategic pivot after acquiring the Crypto.com domain for a reported $5 to $10 million, followed by massiv

ai.com domain sold for $70 million
Summarized by AI from the post below


##
Motivational Quotes
Wealth Logic
· March 17 at 7:17 PM · Shared with Public group
In the early days of the internet, domain names were simple and inexpensive. Anyone could register a short, catchy address for a small fee, often without much thought about future value. In 1993, when the World Wide Web was just becoming public, the domain AI.com was registered on May 4—only days after the web went fully public. Back then, "AI" stood for artificial intelligence, but it was mostly an academic term confined to research papers and labs. No one imagined it would become one of the defining technologies of the 21st century.
The original registration cost was minimal—around $75. Over the decades, the domain changed hands multiple times. It passed through various owners, including a Kuwaiti-owned holding company called Future Media Architects. By 2021, Malaysian tech entrepreneur Arsyan Ismail acquired it through a brokerage site (reportedly for around $10–11 million, though exact figures vary in reports). Ismail, whose initials match "AI," saw potential in the short, two-letter domain.
Two-letter .com domains are extremely rare and highly prized. They are easy to remember, brandable across languages, and carry instant global recognition. As the internet matured, premium domains became digital real estate—valuable assets like prime physical property. But AI.com's true worth exploded with the rise of artificial intelligence.
Starting around 2022–2023, generative AI tools like ChatGPT made "AI" a household term. Companies raced to build AI products, invest billions, and dominate the space. The letters "AI" suddenly represented massive future potential—innovation, automation, intelligence, and trillions in economic impact. Short domains tied to hot trends became goldmines.
In April 2025, Ismail sold AI.com for $70 million, paid in cryptocurrency. The buyer was Kris Marszalek, co-founder and CEO of Crypto.com, a major cryptocurrency exchange. The deal was brokered by domain expert Larry Fischer of GetYourDomain. It shattered records, more than doubling the previous public high of $30 million for Voice.com in 2019 (and surpassing older ones like CarInsurance.com at $49.7 million).
The sale stayed private at first but was publicly confirmed in early 2026, just before the domain relaunched. Marszalek used it to launch an "agentic AI" platform—autonomous AI agents that handle tasks independently for users—teased during Super Bowl LX ads. This timing amplified the story: a domain bought cheaply

results.title: Kyte.com Now Available for Acquisition Through Saw.com
results.text:
Kyte.com Now Available for Acquisition Through Saw.com

 

Table of contents

A Four-Letter .COM That Feels Like a Brand

# Kyte.com Now Available for Acquisition Through Saw.com

Kyte.com is now officially available for acquisition through Saw.com. This premium, four-letter .com domain represents a rare opportunity for companies operating in mobility, AI-powered platforms, travel technology, fintech, creator tools, and modern consumer brands seeking a short, global, brandable identity. Memorable, versatile, and category-flexible, Kyte.com offers immediate authority and long-term strategic value in competitive digital markets.

## A Four-Letter .COM That Feels Like a Brand

In a market crowded with longer names, hyphenated compromises, and alternative extensions, Kyte.com stands out for its clarity and flexibility.

Four-letter .COM domains are finite, with only 456,976 total possible combinations in existence. Today, more than 99% of them are already registered, and the vast majority of short, pronounceable combinations are tightly held by owners who rarely bring them to market.

“Kyte” evokes:

- Mobility & freedom (kite → movement, air, flow)

- Lightness & speed

- Modern tech identity

- Playful but premium consumer branding

It’s short enough for app icons. Strong enough for billboards. Clean enough for global scale.

## Market & Search Data

From a performance standpoint, short .COM domains have been shown to generate up to 33% higher click-through rates compared to longer alternatives, according to industry SEO benchmarks. Strong domains also benefit from direct navigation traffic, which can account for an estimated 15–25% of total branded web traffic. This positioning becomes even more strategic when viewed against the scale of the industries Kyte.com can serve: the global mobility market is projected to exceed $1.3 trillion by 2027, while the artificial intelligence market is forecast to surpass $1.8 trillion by 2030.

﻿

Companies that secure category-defining .com assets benefit from:

- Higher direct navigation traffic

- Increased credibility in investor conversations

- Lower long-term customer acquisition costs

- Stronger brand defensibility

- Clean global positioning

## Strategic Fit Across High-Growth Sectors

Mobility & Transportation The global mobility market continues to expand rapidly, including EV platforms, car-sharing, micro-mobility, and autonomous systems. Kyte.com is an intuitive fit for ride-hailing, subscription vehicles, EV infrastructure, or smart transport solutions.

AI & SaaS Platforms Short, abstract names increasingly dominate AI branding. A name like Kyte works as an umbrella brand for AI tools, data infrastructure, automation platforms, or productivity software.

Travel & Booking Platforms Travel brands benefit from names that signal movement and ease. Kyte.com is memorable, light, and globally pronounceable — ideal for booking engines, travel fintech, or experience marketplaces.

results.title: Howie.ai acquires Howie.com
results.author: Raymond Hackney
results.text:
Howie.ai acquires Howie.com

Austin Petersmith created quite the buzz when last night he posted on X that Howie.com was acquired for $1.2M. Just 15 minutes ago or 15 hours later, he posted this was a joke. Not sure if it was a joke on the price or how is investors reacted? So how much it sold for remains a mystery.

Thanks to Kyle Tully who mentioned on Namepros that the name was available for $960 a month on a lease, (not to own). This was through Venture.com.

my investors when i told them we spent $1.2M to buy howie dot com and all we're doing is redirecting it to our old domain pic.twitter.com/SDeH8yDfl8

> — austin petersmith (@awwstn) March 17, 2025
